questions.question: Explain the seven fields of /etc/passwd file with an example entry.
keyPoints: Username - login name (e.g., john)
Password - traditionally encrypted, now 'x' indicating shadow
UID - User ID number (e.g., 1000)
GID - Group ID number (e.g., 1000)
GECOS - User info like full name, room, phone
Home directory - user's starting directory (e.g., /home/john)
Login shell - program started at login (e.g., /bin/bash)

questions.question: Why is the password field in /etc/passwd set to 'x' in modern Linux systems?
keyPoints: Original password hashes were stored in /etc/passwd
But /etc/passwd is world-readable for system utilities
This made password hashes vulnerable to attacks
Solution: move hashes to /etc/shadow (only root readable)
The 'x' acts as a pointer to /etc/shadow file

questions.question: Differentiate between useradd and usermod commands with their common options.
keyPoints: useradd creates new user, usermod modifies existing user
useradd options: -m (create home), -s (shell), -u (UID), -g (primary group)
usermod options: -l (newname), -d (home), -s (shell), -L (lock), -aG (add groups)
userdel deletes users, userdel -r removes with home directory

questions.question: What is the significance of UID 0 and UID values below 1000?
keyPoints: UID 0 is reserved for root (superuser) with unrestricted privileges
UID 1-99 reserved for system daemons and services
UID 100-999 for pseudo-users created by some services
UID 1000+ are regular user accounts in modern Linux
UID 65534 typically used for 'nobody' user (NFS/unprivileged operations)

questions.question: Explain the purpose and contents of /etc/shadow file.
keyPoints: Stores encrypted password hashes (more secure than passwd)
Contains password aging information (last changed, expiry)
Only readable by root user (unlike world-readable /etc/passwd)
Format: username:password:lastchanged:min:max:warn:inactive:reserved
Enables better security policies with password aging and complexity

questions.question: What are the different types of shells that can be assigned to a user account?
keyPoints: /bin/bash - Bourne Again Shell (most common default)
/bin/sh - Bourne Shell (legacy compatibility)
/bin/false - No shell, prevents login but allows processes
/usr/sbin/nologin - Refuses login with friendly message
/bin/sync - Executes sync then exits (for service accounts)

questions.question: Write the format of /etc/group file and explain each field.
keyPoints: Format: group_name:password:GID:member_list
group_name - name of the group
password - group password (usually empty or 'x')
GID - Group ID number
member_list - comma-separated list of users in group
Multiple groups per user stored across multiple group entries

questions.question: Describe the complete process of user authentication in Linux, including the role of /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, and PAM.
keyPoints: User enters username and password at login prompt
system checks /etc/passwd for valid username and gets UID/GID/shell
system then verifies password against /etc/shadow entry (not /etc/passwd)
PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) provides flexible authentication framework
PAM modules check: password (hash comparison), account status, session setup
On success: set UID, GID, environment, change to home directory, start shell
On failure: deny login with error message

questions.question: Design a step-by-step procedure to create a new user account with specific requirements: UID 1500, primary group 'developers', secondary group ' analysts', home directory /app/user, shell /bin/bash.
keyPoints: Create group if not exists: groupadd developers, groupadd analysts
Create user: useradd -u 1500 -g developers -G analysts -d /app/user -s /bin/bash username
Set password: passwd username
Verify: cat /etc/passwd | grep username
Verify groups: id username
Check home directory creation: ls -ld /app/user
Modify if needed using usermod command

questions.question: Explain the security concerns with /etc/passwd and how modern Linux systems address them.
keyPoints: Original /etc/passwd stored encrypted passwords
File is world-readable (needed for user lookups)
Vulnerability: attackers could steal hashes and crack passwords
Solution 1: Use /etc/shadow (root-only readable)
Solution 2: Use strong password hashing (SHA-512, bcrypt)
Solution 3: Password aging policies (min/max days)
Solution 4: PAM modules for additional checks (failed login lockout)
Modern systems: password field contains 'x', actual hash in /etc/shadow

questions.question: Compare and contrast /etc/passwd and /etc/group files in Linux.
keyPoints: Both are authentication-related configuration files
/etc/passwd: user database, 7 fields per line, all users readable
/etc/group: group database, 4 fields per line, defines group membership
/etc/passwd fields: username:passwd:UID:GID:info:home:shell
/etc/group fields: groupname:passwd:GID:memberlist
Primary group for user in passwd (GID field), secondary groups in group file
Both use ':' as delimiter
Both can be viewed by all users but only root can modify /etc/shadow
